content.text: A single-page application (SPA) is a web application that loads one HTML document on the user's first visit and then updates the content dynamically using JavaScript — without ever requesting a new full HTML page from the server. Navigation feels instant because the browser never reloads: it intercepts link clicks, updates the URL using the History API, and re-renders the relevant part of the page in place.

attrs.alt: Three-column diagram comparing MPA (full server round-trip per page), SPA (one HTML shell, JS handles routing in-place), and SSR+React/Next.js (server HTML on first load, SPA behaviour after hydration)
attrs.title: MPA vs SPA vs SSR+React

content.text: Before SPAs became widespread, every navigation meant a full browser round-trip. The user clicked a link, the browser sent a GET request to the server, the server returned a complete HTML document, and the browser painted the new page — discarding the previous one entirely.

This model works well for content-heavy sites where each page is relatively independent (blogs, e-commerce product pages, documentation). Its strengths are simplicity, built-in SEO (the server delivers complete HTML that crawlers can read), and no dependency on JavaScript. Its weakness is the visible white flash and latency on every navigation.
The key to understanding SPAs is the browser's History API, specifically
. This function lets JavaScript change the URL displayed in the address bar without triggering a network request. Combined with an event listener on
(for the back button), a client-side router can simulate full page navigation entirely within the running JavaScript application.

is handled by a router library (React Router, TanStack Router). When the URL changes, the router component tree resolves to a new set of components to render, and React performs a targeted update — only the changed parts of the DOM are touched.
Browser downloads and executes the JavaScript bundle.

React is the most widely used library for building SPAs, but it is not exclusively a SPA tool. React itself is rendering-strategy agnostic — it has no opinion on whether HTML is generated on the server, the client, or both. What makes React well-suited for SPAs is the component model and the virtual DOM: large, complex interactive UIs composed from reusable components that update efficiently in response to state changes are exactly what SPAs require.
React runs entirely in the browser; server delivers empty HTML shell
React renders HTML on the server; client hydrates to become interactive
React renders HTML at build time; deployed as static files
React added to specific interactive islands in a mostly-server-rendered page
After the initial bundle loads, switching between pages feels immediate — no network round-trip, no white flash.
A music player can keep playing while the user navigates. A form's partially-filled values survive page changes.
Drag-and-drop, real-time collaboration, complex data visualisations — features that would be painful to build with server rendering and form submissions.
Decoupled front end and back end.
The API can be consumed by the web SPA, a mobile app, and third-party integrations simultaneously.
The browser must download, parse, and execute the entire JavaScript bundle before anything meaningful appears. On slow connections or low-power devices this can take seconds.
Search engine crawlers traditionally index only the initial HTML. A pure SPA delivers an almost-empty HTML document, which can hurt indexing unless you add SSR or pre-rendering.
If JavaScript fails to load (ad blocker, network error, JS error), the user may see a blank page.
Long-running SPA sessions can accumulate memory from event listeners and closures that are not properly cleaned up, which is rarely an issue in traditional page-reload apps.

SSR frameworks like Next.js address the SPA's initial load and SEO problems by running React on the server for each request. The server generates complete HTML, sends it to the browser, and the browser can display meaningful content before JavaScript has loaded. Once the JS bundle arrives, React attaches event handlers to the existing HTML nodes — a process called hydration — and the page then behaves like a SPA.

The rendered HTML is sent to the browser as a normal HTTP response. The browser paints it immediately, with no JavaScript required for the initial render. The crawlable, meaningful HTML is also what search engines index — solving the SPA's SEO problem.
There is no single correct choice. The right rendering strategy depends on the nature of the application:
Users are authenticated; SEO irrelevant; rich interactivity needed
SEO critical; content changes infrequently; fast first paint needed
Product pages need SEO; cart and checkout need interactivity
WebSocket state must persist across navigations; no page reload allowed
Content is static; cheapest to host; fastest to serve

React holds the largest market share among JavaScript UI frameworks for SPAs (as of 2025), which means it has by far the largest ecosystem of routing libraries, state management tools, UI component libraries, testing utilities, and deployment targets. This ecosystem advantage is one of the primary reasons teams choose React for SPA development.
shadcn/ui, Radix UI, Material UI, Ant Design.
Vite (de facto standard for new projects), Turbopack.

From roughly 2015 to 2020, client-side SPAs were the default choice for new React applications. Starting around 2021, the React ecosystem has shifted substantially toward SSR-first frameworks, driven by two concerns:

Google began using Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), First Input Delay (FID), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) as search ranking signals. Pure SPAs frequently score poorly on LCP because the browser must execute JavaScript before displaying content.

React 18 introduced a new component model that runs exclusively on the server — with direct database access, no JavaScript bundle cost, and no client-side hydration overhead. This makes SSR frameworks significantly more capable than their 2018 predecessors.

The practical result is that Next.js (with the App Router) has become the recommended starting point for new React projects when SEO or initial load performance matters. Pure Vite SPAs remain the better choice for internal tools and applications where users are authenticated before they see any content.

A key operational difference between SPAs and traditional web applications is the server-side routing requirement. Because a SPA handles routing in the browser, the server must be configured to serve the same
for every URL — otherwise a direct visit to
will result in a 404 from the server.

Hosting platforms like Vercel, Netlify, and Cloudflare Pages handle this automatically when they detect a Vite or Create React App build output — you do not need to configure it manually. However, if you host on a plain static file server, you must configure the catch-all rule explicitly.

The initial bundle size is the SPA's primary performance concern. Code splitting — loading JavaScript for each route only when the user navigates to it — is the standard mitigation. Vite handles this automatically when you use dynamic imports with React's lazy loading API:

With route-based code splitting, the initial bundle contains only the code needed to render the first route. The Reports page bundle, for example, is only downloaded if and when the user navigates to it.
Hydration: How SPAs and SSR Combine

Hydration is the process by which React "takes over" an HTML page that was rendered by a server. The server sends fully-formed HTML; the browser displays it immediately; React's JavaScript then loads, attaches event handlers to the existing DOM nodes, and from that point forward the app behaves as a normal SPA.
Hydration errors occur when the HTML the server rendered does not exactly match what React would render on the client. Common causes:
in a component (different values each call).
) in server-side render context where they are undefined.
Third-party browser extensions that modify the DOM before React attaches.
Static Site Generation: SPAs at Build Time

Static site generation (SSG) is a variant where React runs at build time instead of at request time. The build process visits every route, renders the component tree to HTML, and saves a corresponding
file. Those files are served from a CDN without any server-side code.
Several well-established patterns mitigate the SPA's primary weakness — initial load time.

Modern routers can prefetch a route's JavaScript bundle when the user hovers over a link — so that when they click, the bundle has already loaded. This makes navigation feel instant even though the bundle was fetched just-in-time:

A service worker can cache the application shell (HTML + JS + CSS) after the first visit. On subsequent visits, the service worker serves the app instantly from cache — making the SPA behave almost like a native application, with no network dependency for the UI layer. Data fetching still uses the network, but the UI skeleton loads instantly.

The single most effective way to reduce SPA initial load time is to understand what is in the bundle. Vite's rollup-plugin-visualizer generates an interactive treemap of the bundle, making large dependencies immediately obvious:

# Install the visualizer
npm install --save-dev rollup-plugin-visualizer

# vite.config.ts
import { visualizer } from 'rollup-plugin-visualizer';
export default { plugins: [react(), visualizer({ open: true })] };

# Build — the visualizer opens in your browser automatically
npm run build

Common findings from bundle analysis: an entire icon library imported when only a few icons are needed; a date library (moment.js is 300+ kb unminified) when a lighter alternative (date-fns, dayjs) would work; duplicate dependencies pulled in by two different packages.

Authentication is one of the most important architectural concerns specific to SPAs. Because the browser handles all routing, there is no server-side redirect to a login page — you must implement route protection in the client-side router.

Client-side route protection is a UX guard, not a security boundary. The actual security boundary is your API — every API endpoint must verify authentication independently, regardless of what the client-side router shows or hides.
Understanding what can go wrong in a SPA helps you build one that performs and behaves correctly from the start.
No catch-all route on the server
The most common deployment mistake: forgetting to configure the server to return
for unrecognised paths. A user who bookmarks
and returns directly will get a 404. This is purely a server configuration issue, not a React issue.
Fetching data in every component independently

In an MPA, each page is a fresh request so duplicate data fetching is not a concern. In a SPA, fetching the same user profile in three different components creates three network requests and three loading spinners in different parts of the UI. Use a request-deduplication layer (TanStack Query, SWR, or React Context) to share the fetched result across components.

In a traditional MPA, a page reload clears all JavaScript state and cancels pending requests — navigation is the cleanup. In a SPA, components unmount without a page reload, so any pending fetch, timer, or event listener attached during mount must be explicitly cancelled in the effect's cleanup function. Failing to do this causes state updates on unmounted components and can produce memory leaks in long-running sessions.
Blocking navigation on unsaved changes without confirmation

In an MPA, the browser automatically shows a "Leave page?" dialog when the user has unsaved form changes and clicks a link. In a SPA, client-side navigation bypasses this browser mechanism. You must implement the block yourself using the router's navigation-blocking API:
The choice between a SPA and a server-rendered approach is primarily driven by four factors:
If the UI requires real-time updates, complex drag-and-drop, or state that must persist across many pages without a reload, a SPA is the natural fit.
If content must be indexed by search engines, SSR or SSG is strongly preferred. Pure SPAs can be indexed but require additional investment.
Authenticated-only applications (SaaS tools, admin panels) rarely need SEO — a SPA is perfectly appropriate. Public-facing content sites are the opposite.
SSR introduces complexity around hydration, server deployment, and caching. A team comfortable with backend Node.js can absorb this; a purely frontend team may prefer a simpler SPA + separate API.

In 2025, the practical answer for most new React projects is: start with Next.js (SSR + SPA behaviour post-hydration). It costs little extra for simple apps but removes the SEO and initial-load constraints if the project scope expands.
An SPA loads one HTML shell and updates content in-place using JavaScript, with the browser's History API managing URLs without full reloads.
SPAs excel at rich interactive applications (dashboards, tools); they struggle with initial load time and SEO.
React is rendering-strategy agnostic — it powers pure SPAs, SSR apps, and statically-generated sites equally well.
SSR frameworks (Next.js, Remix) bridge the gap: server-rendered HTML for first load, SPA behaviour after hydration.
SPA deployments require a server catch-all rule so that direct URL access returns
) reduces initial bundle size by loading each page only when needed.
